entry.title: Azure Batch
entry.description: Azure Batch provides cloud-scale job scheduling and compute management for parallel and high-performance computing workloads.

freeTierDetails.summary: 750 hours per month of low-priority Standard_A1 v2 VMs free.
limits: Standard storage and networking charges apply
caveats: Only low-priority VMs are free; other resources billed separately
Free tier does not include premium support
billingRiskNotes: Exceeding free VM hours or using non-eligible VM sizes incurs charges
Storage and outbound data transfer may generate costs

quickstartSteps: Sign up for an Azure account and activate the free tier.
Create a Batch account in the Azure portal.
Configure a pool with eligible low-priority VMs.
Upload your application and input data to Azure Storage.
Define and submit jobs and tasks to the Batch account.
Monitor job progress and retrieve output upon completion.
entry.whenToUse: Use Azure Batch for automating large-scale parallel jobs or high-performance computing workloads without managing infrastructure.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you need real-time processing, persistent compute, or require VM types not covered by the free tier.
entry.body: Azure Batch enables you to run large-scale parallel and high-performance computing jobs in the cloud, automating resource management and job scheduling.

entry.title: Azure Container Apps
entry.description: Azure Container Apps lets you run microservices and containerized apps without managing infrastructure.

freeTierDetails.summary: Includes 180,000 vCPU-seconds, 360,000 GiB-seconds, and 2 million requests per month free.
caveats: Free tier applies to consumption plan only
Networking and storage may incur extra costs
billingRiskNotes: Exceeding free limits incurs pay-as-you-go charges

quickstartSteps: Sign in to the Azure Portal.
Create a new Container Apps environment.
Deploy your container image from Docker Hub or Azure Container Registry.
Configure scaling and ingress settings.
Test your app endpoint.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need to deploy containerized apps without managing Kubernetes or underlying infrastructure.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require full Kubernetes control, persistent storage, or need advanced networking features.
entry.body: Azure Container Apps provides a serverless platform for running containerized applications and microservices, with a generous free tier for small workloads.

entry.title: Azure Container Registry
entry.description: Azure Container Registry provides private Docker container image storage, management, and geo-replication for Azure deployments.

freeTierDetails.summary: Free tier includes one managed registry with limited storage and image operations.
limits: Unlimited image pulls/pushes (subject to bandwidth limits)
caveats: Geo-replication and premium features not included
Bandwidth and storage overages incur charges
billingRiskNotes: Exceeding storage or bandwidth limits may result in charges

useCases: Store and manage private Docker images
quickstartSteps: Sign in to the Azure Portal.
Create a new Container Registry resource.
Set the SKU to 'Standard' for free tier eligibility.
Push Docker images to your registry using Azure CLI.
Configure access permissions for your team or CI/CD pipeline.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need private, managed Docker image storage tightly integrated with Azure services and workflows.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require unlimited storage, advanced geo-replication, or want a registry without Azure account or credit card.
entry.body: Azure Container Registry offers a managed, private Docker registry service for storing and managing container images, with seamless Azure integration and a free tier suitable for small teams or development.

entry.title: Azure Functions
entry.description: Azure Functions is a serverless compute service for running event-driven code without managing infrastructure.

freeTierDetails.summary: 1 million free requests and 400,000 GB-s compute per month.
caveats: Premium and Dedicated plans not included
Some triggers may incur additional costs
billingRiskNotes: Overages billed per request and GB-s beyond free tier

quickstartSteps: Sign in to the Azure Portal.
Create a new Function App resource.
Choose a runtime stack (e.g., Node.js, Python, .NET).
Create a new function using the portal or VS Code extension.
Configure a trigger (HTTP, timer, queue, etc.) and deploy your code.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need to run code in response to events or HTTP requests without managing servers.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require long-running processes, custom OS dependencies, or predictable dedicated compute resources.
entry.body: Azure Functions enables you to build and deploy event-driven applications with minimal setup, offering a generous free tier for lightweight and scalable workloads.

entry.title: Azure Kubernetes Service
entry.description: Managed Kubernetes clusters with integrated scaling, monitoring, and security on Azure.

freeTierDetails.summary: Free tier includes no charge for cluster management and one free node pool with Linux nodes.
limits: One free node pool with Linux nodes
caveats: You pay for VM instances and storage used by your nodes.
Windows node pools are not included in the free tier.
billingRiskNotes: VM and storage charges can accrue quickly if not monitored.

quickstartSteps: Sign in to the Azure Portal.
Create a new Kubernetes Service (AKS) cluster.
Configure node pool and networking settings.
Deploy your containerized app using kubectl.
Monitor and scale your cluster as needed.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need a managed Kubernetes cluster with integrated Azure services and simplified scaling for containerized workloads.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require a fully free solution or want to avoid VM and storage charges, or need support for non-Azure environments.
entry.body: Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) provides a managed Kubernetes environment for deploying, managing, and scaling containerized applications with integrated Azure features.

entry.title: Azure VM Image Builder
entry.description: Azure VM Image Builder automates creation, customization, and management of virtual machine images at scale in Azure.

freeTierDetails.summary: Free tier includes 20 custom image builds per month.
caveats: Standard Azure VM and storage costs apply for resources used during image creation.
billingRiskNotes: Charges may occur if you exceed the free build limit or for underlying VM/storage usage.

quickstartSteps: Sign in to the Azure Portal and ensure you have a subscription.
Register the Image Builder resource provider in your subscription.
Create an Image Template specifying source image, customizations, and output.
Assign required permissions to the Image Builder managed identity.
Start the image build process and monitor status in the portal.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need to automate and standardize VM image creation for Azure deployments.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require image building for non-Azure clouds or need advanced customization not supported by Azure.
avoidIf: You have highly specialized build requirements
entry.body: Azure VM Image Builder enables automated, repeatable creation of customized VM images, supporting compliance and DevOps workflows for Azure environments.

entry.title: Cloud Functions
entry.description: Google Cloud Functions is a serverless compute service for running event-driven code without managing servers.

freeTierDetails.summary: Always-free tier includes generous monthly limits for invocations, compute time, and networking.
caveats: Limits apply per billing account, not per project.
Outbound data over 5GB incurs charges.
billingRiskNotes: Overages are billed automatically if limits are exceeded.

quickstartSteps: Sign in to Google Cloud Console and create a project.
Enable the Cloud Functions API.
Install and configure the Google Cloud SDK.
Write your function code and deploy using the gcloud CLI.
Test your function by triggering an event or HTTP request.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need to run code in response to events without managing servers, especially for lightweight APIs or automation.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid for long-running processes, heavy compute workloads, or when you need fine-grained control over infrastructure.
entry.body: Google Cloud Functions offers a generous always-free tier for running event-driven serverless code, ideal for lightweight APIs, automation, and prototyping. It is easy to set up and integrates well with other Google Cloud services, but may not suit long-running or resource-intensive workloads.

entry.title: Cloud Run
entry.description: Cloud Run is a fully managed serverless platform for running containerized applications on Google Cloud.

freeTierDetails.summary: Cloud Run offers a generous always-free tier with monthly resource limits.
caveats: Free tier applies only to one region per billing account
Outbound data transfer beyond 1 GiB is billed
billingRiskNotes: Usage above free limits is automatically billed
No hard cap; monitor usage to avoid unexpected charges

quickstartSteps: Install and configure the Google Cloud CLI.
Containerize your application using Docker.
Push your container image to Google Container Registry or Artifact Registry.
Deploy the container to Cloud Run via the Google Cloud Console or CLI.
Test your deployed service using the provided HTTPS endpoint.
entry.whenToUse: Use Cloud Run when you need to deploy containerized applications without managing servers or infrastructure, especially for stateless workloads.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid Cloud Run if your application requires persistent local storage, stateful workloads, or specialized networking beyond HTTP(S).
entry.body: Cloud Run enables you to deploy and scale containerized applications easily, paying only for resources used, with a generous always-free tier for low-traffic workloads.

entry.title: Cloudflare Workers
entry.description: Cloudflare Workers is a serverless platform for deploying JavaScript, TypeScript, and WASM code at the edge.

freeTierDetails.summary: Free tier includes up to 100,000 requests per day with limited CPU time per request.
caveats: No custom domains on free tier
Limited to 10ms CPU per request

quickstartSteps: Sign up for a Cloudflare account.
Install the Wrangler CLI tool.
Run 'wrangler generate my-worker' to scaffold a new project.
Edit the generated code to implement your logic.
Deploy using 'wrangler publish'.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need fast, globally distributed serverless functions for lightweight APIs, edge logic, or request handling.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you need long-running processes, large memory, or advanced networking features unavailable in Workers.
entry.body: Cloudflare Workers enables you to deploy serverless code at the edge with minimal setup, ideal for APIs, webhooks, and lightweight backend logic.

entry.title: Cloudflare Workers KV
entry.description: Cloudflare Workers KV is a globally distributed key-value storage for serverless applications running on Cloudflare Workers.

freeTierDetails.summary: Free tier includes 1 GB storage, 100,000 read operations, and 1,000 write/delete operations per day.
caveats: Eventual consistency
Per-account limits apply
billingRiskNotes: Hard caps prevent unexpected charges

quickstartSteps: Sign up for a Cloudflare account.
Install Wrangler CLI and authenticate.
Create a new Worker project with Wrangler.
Add a KV namespace in your Cloudflare dashboard.
Bind the KV namespace to your Worker in wrangler.toml.
Deploy your Worker and interact with KV storage.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need low-latency, globally distributed key-value storage for serverless or edge applications.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require strong consistency, complex queries, or large object storage beyond 25 MB per value.
entry.body: Cloudflare Workers KV provides globally distributed, eventually consistent key-value storage for serverless applications, ideal for edge use cases with simple data access patterns.

entry.title: Compute Engine e2-micro
entry.description: Google Cloud's always-free virtual machine instance for lightweight compute workloads and prototyping.

freeTierDetails.summary: 1 e2-micro VM instance per month in select regions, always free within usage limits.
limits: 30 GB HDD or 10 GB SSD storage
1 GB outbound traffic per month (North America regions)
caveats: Only certain US regions eligible
Linux OS only
Network egress limits apply
billingRiskNotes: Charges apply if limits exceeded or non-eligible regions used

quickstartSteps: Sign up for a Google Cloud account and verify billing.
Navigate to Compute Engine in the Google Cloud Console.
Create a new VM instance, selecting e2-micro machine type and eligible region.
Choose a supported Linux OS image.
Configure firewall and storage settings as needed, then launch the instance.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need a free, low-resource VM for development, testing, or learning cloud basics in eligible US regions.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid for production workloads, high-availability needs, or if you require more resources, Windows OS, or global regions.
entry.body: Compute Engine e2-micro provides a free, always-available VM instance suitable for learning, prototyping, and lightweight workloads in select US regions.

entry.title: EMQX Serverless
entry.description: A fully managed, serverless MQTT messaging service for IoT and real-time applications.

freeTierDetails.summary: Free tier includes 1 million messages and 100 connected devices per month.
caveats: Overages incur charges
Limited to 1 deployment
billingRiskNotes: Usage beyond free limits is billed automatically

quickstartSteps: Sign up for an EMQX Cloud account.
Navigate to the Serverless MQTT section.
Create a new serverless deployment.
Configure MQTT connection settings.
Connect your devices or applications using the provided endpoint.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need scalable, managed MQTT messaging for IoT or real-time apps without managing infrastructure.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require protocols beyond MQTT, need on-premises deployment, or expect to exceed free tier limits frequently.
entry.body: EMQX Serverless provides a managed MQTT broker with a generous free tier, ideal for IoT and real-time messaging projects without infrastructure overhead.

entry.title: Firebase Cloud Functions
entry.description: Run backend code in response to events triggered by Firebase features and HTTPS requests, without managing servers.

freeTierDetails.summary: Generous always-free tier for invocations, compute time, and outbound networking.
caveats: Outbound networking limit applies to non-Google services.
Cold starts may affect latency.
billingRiskNotes: Exceeding free limits incurs charges.
No hard cap; monitor usage to avoid unexpected costs.

useCases: Trigger backend logic on Firestore/Realtime Database changes
Build REST APIs for mobile/web apps
quickstartSteps: Install Firebase CLI and initialize your project with 'firebase init functions'.
Write your function code in 'functions/index.js' or 'functions/src/index.ts'.
Deploy your functions using 'firebase deploy --only functions'.
Test your functions via Firebase console or HTTP requests.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need to run backend code in response to Firebase events or HTTP requests without managing infrastructure.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require long-running processes, custom runtimes, or need strict control over server resources and networking.
entry.body: Firebase Cloud Functions lets you run backend code in response to Firebase events or HTTP requests, with a generous free tier and seamless integration with other Firebase services.

entry.title: Google Kubernetes Engine
entry.description: Google Kubernetes Engine provides managed Kubernetes clusters for deploying, managing, and scaling containerized applications on Google Cloud.

freeTierDetails.summary: One zonal GKE cluster per month with no cluster management fee.
limits: Compute, storage, and network resources billed separately
caveats: Node VM and storage usage incur standard charges
Free tier covers only cluster management fee
billingRiskNotes: Resource usage beyond cluster management incurs charges

quickstartSteps: Sign up for a Google Cloud account and enable billing.
Enable the Kubernetes Engine API in your project.
Install and configure the Google Cloud CLI.
Create a zonal GKE cluster using the Cloud Console or CLI.
Deploy your containerized application to the cluster.
entry.whenToUse: Use GKE when you need a managed Kubernetes environment for deploying and scaling containerized workloads on Google Cloud.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid GKE if you require a fully free solution or want to avoid any compute or storage charges.
entry.body: Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) offers a managed Kubernetes service with an always-free tier covering cluster management fees for one zonal cluster per month. While the management fee is waived, you are still billed for the underlying compute, storage, and network resources used by your workloads. GKE is ideal for teams deploying containerized applications at scale, but not suitable if you require a completely free hosting solution.

entry.title: Khan Academy
entry.description: Khan Academy offers free interactive coding lessons and exercises for beginners in JavaScript, HTML/CSS, and SQL.

freeTierDetails.summary: All programming courses and exercises are free with no time limits or payment required.
caveats: No advanced or professional-level content
No official certification

quickstartSteps: Go to https://www.khanacademy.org/computing/computer-programming.
Sign up for a free Khan Academy account or log in.
Choose a programming course (e.g., JavaScript, HTML/CSS, SQL).
Start the first lesson and complete interactive exercises.
Track your progress and continue through the curriculum.
entry.whenToUse: Use Khan Academy to learn programming fundamentals interactively, especially if you are a beginner or need structured, self-paced lessons.
entry.whenNotToUse: Do not use if you need advanced programming topics, professional certifications, or project-based portfolio development.
entry.body: Khan Academy provides free, interactive programming courses for beginners, covering JavaScript, HTML/CSS, and SQL with hands-on exercises and immediate feedback.

entry.title: Lambda
entry.description: AWS Lambda runs code in response to events without provisioning or managing servers.

freeTierDetails.summary: 1M free requests and 400,000 GB-seconds compute time per month.
caveats: Limits apply per AWS account, not per function.
Charges apply for usage above free tier.
billingRiskNotes: Overages are billed automatically if limits are exceeded.

quickstartSteps: Sign in to the AWS Management Console.
Navigate to AWS Lambda and click 'Create function'.
Choose 'Author from scratch', enter a function name, and select a runtime.
Write or upload your function code.
Configure a trigger (e.g., API Gateway, S3 event).
Deploy and test your Lambda function.
entry.whenToUse: Use Lambda for event-driven workloads, APIs, or automation where you want to avoid server management and scale automatically.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid Lambda for long-running processes, heavy compute workloads, or when you need predictable, sustained performance.
entry.body: AWS Lambda lets you run code in response to events without managing servers, with a generous free tier suitable for many workloads and easy scaling.

entry.title: Oracle Always Free Compute
entry.description: Oracle Cloud offers always-free virtual machines and resources for lightweight compute workloads and development.

freeTierDetails.summary: Always-free tier includes 2 AMD or Arm-based VMs, block storage, and load balancing.
caveats: Limited to specific VM shapes and regions
Performance is basic and not suitable for heavy workloads
billingRiskNotes: No charges unless explicitly upgrading to paid resources

quickstartSteps: Sign up for an Oracle Cloud Free Tier account with a valid credit card.
Log in to the Oracle Cloud Console and navigate to Compute > Instances.
Click 'Create Instance', select an Always Free eligible shape (e.g., VM.Standard.E2.1.Micro or VM.Standard.A1.Flex).
Configure networking and storage as needed within free limits.
Launch the instance and connect via SSH using the provided public IP.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need always-free, basic VMs for development, prototyping, or learning without ongoing costs.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require high performance, advanced networking, or more resources than the free tier allows.
entry.body: Oracle Always Free Compute provides two small virtual machines and essential storage at no cost, ideal for learning, prototyping, and lightweight workloads.

entry.title: Supabase Edge Functions
entry.description: Supabase Edge Functions let you run TypeScript or JavaScript serverless functions close to your users.

freeTierDetails.summary: Free tier includes 500,000 function invocations per month.
caveats: Shared compute resources
Cold starts may occur
billingRiskNotes: Usage above free tier is blocked unless you upgrade

quickstartSteps: Sign up for a free Supabase account.
Create a new project in the Supabase dashboard.
Install the Supabase CLI and initialize functions in your project directory.
Write your TypeScript or JavaScript function in the 'functions' folder.
Deploy your function using the Supabase CLI.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need to deploy lightweight serverless APIs or event-driven logic close to your Supabase project.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you require long-running processes, advanced compute resources, or need to run non-JS/TS code.
entry.body: Supabase Edge Functions provide a simple way to run serverless TypeScript or JavaScript code at the edge, tightly integrated with your Supabase project.

entry.title: Virtual Machines
entry.description: Azure Virtual Machines provides scalable, on-demand compute resources for running applications and workloads in the cloud.

freeTierDetails.summary: 750 hours per month of B1S Windows or Linux VM for 12 months.
caveats: Only select VM sizes eligible
Standard SSD storage included up to 64GB
After 12 months, standard rates apply
billingRiskNotes: Charges apply if usage exceeds free tier or after 12 months

quickstartSteps: Sign up for an Azure account with the free tier.
Navigate to the Azure Portal and select 'Create a resource'.
Choose 'Virtual Machine' and select the B1S size.
Configure OS, networking, and storage as needed.
Review and create the VM.
Connect to your VM using SSH or RDP.
entry.whenToUse: Use when you need flexible, on-demand compute for development, testing, or learning cloud infrastructure with minimal upfront cost.
entry.whenNotToUse: Avoid if you need always-free compute, require large-scale production workloads, or want to avoid entering payment details.
entry.body: Azure Virtual Machines offers a 12-month free tier for B1S VMs, ideal for development, testing, and learning cloud computing basics.
